perturbation.original_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of twelve (12) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements.
perturbation.changed_text: In consideration of the Confidential Information being furnished hereby, each party agrees that, without the prior written consent of the Chief Financial Officer or General Counsel of the other party, for a period of six (6) months from the date of this Agreement neither it nor any of its affiliates will, either directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, or otherwise contract for the services of (or cause or seek to cause to leave the employ of other party or any of its affiliates) any person who is now employed or engaged (either as an employee or consultant) or becomes employed or engaged during the term of this Agreement by the other party or its affiliates, other than persons whose employment or engagement shall have been terminated at least six (6) months prior to the date of such solicitation, employment or other contractual arrangements.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period from 12 months to 6 months may be unreasonably short and thus be considered an unenforceable restraint on trade under California law, given the context of access to confidential information. The reasonableness of a non-solicitation agreement is fact-dependent, but California courts often disfavor overly broad restrictions, particularly given California's strong policy in favor of employee mobility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain anyone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrow restraints to protect trade secrets are sometimes permitted, a six-month non-solicitation clause, especially given the access to confidential information, could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade, depending on the specific facts and the employee's role, thus violating the spirit and potentially the letter of Section 16600.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-solicitation period from 12 months to 6 months. The Perturbation and Law Explanations both state that, especially in the context of confidential information, this shorter period 'could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade', potentially violating California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void'. The reduction in time makes the restraint on trade potentially unreasonable and therefore contradicts the law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall be one (1) year from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall be five (5) years from the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the agreement term to five years could be deemed unreasonable and therefore unenforceable, particularly in California, given the potential impact on competition and the lack of a clear justification for such a long duration in an NDA related to a potential transaction. California law favors competition and disfavors contracts that restrain trade.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally voids contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A five-year term for a non-disclosure agreement, especially concerning information related to a potential transaction that may or may not occur, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade, making it potentially unenforceable under California law.  The reasonableness depends on the specific facts, but the length itself raises concerns.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the agreement term to five years, which raises concerns about violating California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally voids contracts that restrain trade. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The Law Explanation also supports this, stating that a five-year term for a non-disclosure agreement could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint on trade. The original text's one-year term is less likely to be viewed as an unreasonable restraint compared to the modified five-year term. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Recipient shall promptly destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials except that (i) one copy of each electronic version of such materials may be retained by Recipient and its Representatives in their archives pursuant to such party’s standard data backup and disaster recovery plans and (ii) Recipient’s outside legal counsel and accountants may retain one hard copy of such materials in its archives solely for regulatory or corporate records retention policy compliance and for dispute resolution; provided, however, that any Confidential Information contained in such format may not be used for any other purpose and that all obligations contained herein shall, with respect to any Confidential Information so retained, survive any termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s written request, the Recipient shall destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all written Confidential Information (whether prepared by the Disclosing party or its Representatives) and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written materials. No exceptions are made for retaining copies.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the exception for retaining copies for legal counsel and accountants could violate professional rules of conduct that require lawyers to maintain records of their work. Attorneys in California have an ethical duty to retain client files under certain circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 1.15
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 1.15 Cal. Rules of Prof. Conduct
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 1.15 of the California Rules of Professional Conduct addresses safeguarding client property. While it doesn't explicitly mandate retaining all documents, it implies that attorneys must take reasonable steps to protect client interests, which may include retaining certain documents, especially for ongoing representation or potential disputes. Eliminating the ability to retain copies, especially for legal counsel, could create a situation where the attorney is unable to adequately represent their client's interests, potentially violating Rule 1.15.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Page Not Found Search Search Website We’re sorry. The page you requested is not available. See if you can find the information you need on our home page . You can also use search to find it by clicking on the magnifying glass at the top righthand side of the page.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the exceptions allowing the Recipient's legal counsel and accountants to retain copies of confidential information, directly contradicting the professional rules of conduct that may require lawyers to maintain records. The Law Explanation indicates that eliminating the ability to retain copies could create a situation where the attorney is unable to adequately represent their client's interests, potentially violating Rule 1.15. The Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition indicates a contradiction exists when a contract sets a requirement that violates federal or state law. In this instance, the change creates a contract that forces attorneys to violate the California Rules of Professional Conduct.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby acknowledge that they are aware that the United States securities laws prohibit any person who has received from an issuer material, non-public information of the type which is the subject of this Agreement from purchasing or selling securities of such issuer or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person will purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties hereby acknowledge that they are aware that the United States securities laws permit any person who has received from an issuer material, non-public information of the type which is the subject of this Agreement to purchase or sell securities of such issuer or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person will purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'prohibit' to 'permit' directly contradicts insider trading laws, which are designed to prevent the unfair use of non-public information for personal gain in securities trading. This change would make the contract language encourage illegal activity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.  Insider trading, which is the use of material, non-public information for trading securities, falls under this prohibition. The modified text directly contradicts this by stating that the parties acknowledge that securities laws *permit* such actions, which is false and encourages a violation of federal law.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts established securities laws. By changing "prohibit" to "permit," the revised contract language implies that insider trading is allowed, which violates Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5, as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, addresses "manipulative and deceptive devices," under which insider trading falls. The original text correctly acknowledged the prohibition, whereas the changed text now contradicts that fundamental legal principle.
